items.title: Three-way merge — why some merges conflict and others don't
items.mermaid:
flowchart TB
  A["Ancestor: timeout = 10"]
  A --> B["You: timeout = 30 (changed)"]
  A --> C["Them: timeout = 60 (changed)"]
  B & C --> Conflict{"Both changed from same baseline?"}
  Conflict -- "yes & to different values" --> X["CONFLICT — needs human"]
  Conflict -- "no, only one side changed" --> Y["Auto-merge: take the changed side"]
items.caption: Git can only auto-merge when at most one side changed a given region. When both did, it asks you.

items.title: Resolution workflow
items.mermaid:
flowchart LR
  S["git merge / rebase"] --> C{"Conflict?"}
  C -- no --> Done["merge commit made / rebase continues"]
  C -- yes --> St["git status: list conflicted files"]
  St --> Edit["Edit files: replace marker blocks with correct content"]
  Edit --> Add["git add <file>"]
  Add --> Cont["git commit / git rebase --continue"]
  Edit -.bail.-> Abort["git merge --abort / git rebase --abort"]
items.caption: Conflicts pause the operation; you edit, stage, then continue (or abort and try a different approach).
